medications.drug_name: AV-nodal blocking drugs (adenosine, verapamil/diltiazem, digoxin, beta-blockers) - for home self-treatment of a future episode
medications.indication_rationale: These AV-nodal blocking agents must NOT be recommended for home self-treatment: if the patient develops pre-excited atrial fibrillation, blocking the AV node can accelerate conduction down the accessory pathway and precipitate ventricular fibrillation and sudden death. Any acute rhythm treatment must be decided in a monitored in-person setting after the ECG and specialist assessment, not prescribed or self-administered over telehealth.
contraindications_in_this_case: Contraindicated in pre-excited atrial fibrillation (can accelerate accessory-pathway conduction and precipitate ventricular fibrillation)
Not to be recommended for remote/home self-treatment before ECG confirmation and specialist assessment of the accessory pathway

medications.drug_name: energy drinks / pre-workout stimulants / excess caffeine (trigger to eliminate)
medications.indication_rationale: Concentrated stimulants lower the arrhythmia threshold and can precipitate episodes; they are a trigger to eliminate rather than a therapy.
contraindications_in_this_case: Cease all energy drinks and pre-workout supplements and reduce caffeine to lower episode frequency

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Treating the episodes as benign anxiety and doing nothing further (no ECG, no referral)
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Recurrent switch-like presyncopal palpitations need a priority ECG and specialist review to capture pre-excitation; dismissing them as anxiety risks missing WPW and a dangerous future arrhythmia.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: For a future episode, attempt a coached modified Valsalva manoeuvre (bear down against a closed airway for ~15 seconds while lying, then lie flat and raise the legs); if it does not stop the racing, or if you feel faint or develop chest pain, call 000.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: A modified Valsalva is a safe, effective first-line self-help step for a future stable episode of paroxysmal SVT, while clear escalation criteria protect against a dangerous non-terminating or unstable episode.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop all energy drinks and pre-workout supplements and cut down caffeine.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Eliminating concentrated stimulants reduces the frequency of future episodes.

patient_education_points.point: The pattern you describe - a fast, regular racing heart that switches on and off suddenly, with near-fainting, happening several times this year - suggests an 'electrical short-circuit' in the heart, possibly from a tiny extra electrical pathway you may have been born with. Because it stopped on its own you're safe right now, but you need a priority heart tracing (12-lead ECG) to look for a specific pattern, and a heart specialist review.

For a future episode, you can try a simple bearing-down/breathing manoeuvre (a modified Valsalva) to try to reset the rhythm. Please do NOT take any heart-rhythm medication on your own to stop an episode - some common rhythm drugs can be dangerous if this turns out to be a particular type of extra pathway, so any medicine needs to be decided by a doctor after the tracing.

Call 000 for an ambulance if a future episode does not stop within about 15 minutes, if you actually faint/lose consciousness, if the racing feels chaotic/irregular rather than regular, or if you get severe crushing chest pain.

Stopping energy drinks and pre-workout supplements and cutting caffeine will help reduce how often this happens. If the tracing confirms an extra pathway, a specialist can often permanently cure it with a minimally invasive catheter ablation procedure.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent (same-day/next-morning) GP or urgent-care review to obtain a priority resting 12-lead ECG; cardiology/electrophysiology referral for assessment and potential ablation
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: The resting 12-lead ECG for a pre-excitation pattern (short PR, delta wave, widened QRS), the need for ambulatory/event monitoring if the resting ECG is normal, cardiology/EP assessment for accessory-pathway ablation, trigger avoidance, and safety-netting for future episodes
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: A future episode that does not self-terminate, actual syncope, a fast irregular rhythm, or severe chest pain - call 000 immediately.

must_include_items: Recognition of probable paroxysmal SVT / pre-excitation (WPW) from the switch-like recurrent presyncopal history, not dismissal as anxiety
Arranging a priority resting 12-lead ECG to look for a pre-excitation pattern, plus cardiology/electrophysiology referral
Coaching a safe modified Valsalva for a future episode AND explicitly NOT recommending any AV-nodal blocking self-medication
Clear 000 safety-netting for a non-terminating episode, true syncope, a fast irregular rhythm, or severe chest pain
acceptable_alternatives: 'Paroxysmal SVT', 'an electrical short-circuit / extra electrical pathway causing a fast rhythm', 'pre-excitation' are acceptable framings
A same-day GP or urgent-care review to obtain the priority ECG, or a rapid-access arrhythmia/cardiology clinic, are acceptable pathways
errors_of_omission: Not recognising paroxysmal SVT/pre-excitation and attributing it solely to anxiety/panic or caffeine
Not arranging a priority ECG or specialist referral
Not coaching a vagal manoeuvre for future episodes
No 000 safety-netting for a dangerous future episode
errors_of_commission: Recommending an AV-nodal blocking drug (adenosine, verapamil/diltiazem, digoxin, beta-blocker) for home self-treatment - dangerous if pre-excited AF develops
Prescribing any antiarrhythmic remotely before ECG and specialist assessment
Falsely reassuring that the episodes are benign anxiety and no work-up is needed because they self-resolve
Telling the patient there is nothing to do now that it has stopped
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (1) recognising probable paroxysmal SVT/pre-excitation and arranging a priority resting ECG plus cardiology/EP referral, (2) coaching a modified Valsalva for a future episode while explicitly NOT recommending AV-nodal blocking self-medication, and (3) clear 000 safety-netting for a non-terminating/syncopal/irregular/chest-pain recurrence. Recommending an AV-nodal blocker for home self-treatment, prescribing an antiarrhythmic remotely, or dismissing recurrent switch-like presyncopal palpitations as anxiety are failures.
